critical: Today is $holidayName (Yom Tov — a festival day). Please put away your device and keep the day holy. Melacha (forbidden labor) applies on Yom Tov similar to Shabbat.

$holidayName is about to begin. Please finish what you are doing, turn off your phone, and prepare to welcome this Yom Tov with joy.

Our Sages teach that rejoicing on Yom Tov is itself a mitzvah — may your observance bring blessing and divine reward to you and your household.

Today is $name — $meaning

Minor fast rules:
• No eating or drinking from dawn until nightfall.
• Washing, music, and showering are generally permitted (unlike Tisha B'Av and Yom Kippur).
• Special selichot and prayers may be recited in synagogue — check your community schedule.

Read this today (Friday) before Shabbat candles — the app is not for use on Shabbat.

Tomorrow is Shabbat and erev $tomorrowChag. $tomorrowChag begins tomorrow night at nightfall (Motzei Shabbat), not tonight. Finish Yaknehaz prep, Yom Tov candles from a pre-existing flame, wine, and festive food before Shabbat ends.

If you forgot at Maariv on Rosh Chodesh:
• Still in Retzei before God's name at the conclusion — insert Yaaleh V'yavo there and continue ($YAALEH_VYAVO_HALACHA_SOURCE).
• Once you finished Retzei, or after the entire Amidah — do not go back and do not repeat. Beit Din sanctified the new month by day, not at night (Berachot 30b; $YAALEH_VYAVO_HALACHA_SOURCE). Continue davening.

If you forgot:
• Still in Retzei (before concluding the blessing) — insert Yaaleh V'yavo in its place and continue ($YAALEH_VYAVO_HALACHA_SOURCE).
• After concluding Retzei — return to the beginning of Retzei, insert Yaaleh V'yavo, and complete the remaining blessings ($YAALEH_VYAVO_HALACHA_SOURCE).
• Finished the entire Amidah (after the final Yihiyu L'ratzon) — repeat only the $service Amidah (Shemoneh Esrei), never the full service, even if you already davened Musaf, Maariv, or anything else afterward ($YAALEH_VYAVO_HALACHA_SOURCE).

Seder (first night):
• $sederWhen
• Matzah, maror, four cups of wine, reading the Haggadah, afikoman.
• Reclining (hasebha): Recline to the left when drinking the four cups and eating matzah, korech, and afikoman — do not recline while eating maror or chazeret (they symbolize slavery).
• Kiddush, festive meal, Hallel, Nirtzah.

Tomorrow by day:
• Yom Tov davening with Full Hallel and Musaf; no chametz or kitniyot (per your custom).
• Only eat food prepared for Pesach in kosher-for-Passover utensils.

Sefirat HaOmer links Pesach to Shavuot — counting each day from the Exodus toward receiving the Torah.

Today in the Omer: $todaySummary (day $day of 49).

Tonight's count:
• $tonight night — count $todaySummary after nightfall$timePart.
$nextNightLine

How to count:
• Stand and recite the blessing before counting if you are still saying it with a blessing (if you missed a day, ask your rabbi before continuing with a bracha).
• Say: "${omerCountSpeechPhrase(day)}"
• Count after nightfall (tzeit); complete before dawn. If you forgot at night, count the next day during the daytime without a bracha. If you do this before sunset, you can continue counting on subsequent nights with a bracha. You only lose the blessing permanently if you miss an entire 24-hour cycle (both night and the following day) — ask your rav.

$nusachWhen

Sukkot (first day) begins tonight — Zman Simchateinu.

Before sunset:
• Avoid eating a formal meal inside the sukkah today (Rama O.C. 639:1) so that your entry tonight is distinctly dedicated to the start of the mitzvah.
• Have arba minim ready: lulav, etrog, hadasim, aravot (per your rabbi's kashrut standards).

Tonight & tomorrow:
• Light Yom Tov candles in the sukkah (per custom) or home.
${shehecheyanuErevLines(HebrewCalendarEngine.SUCCOS, tomorrowCal, profile)}
• ${if (profile.isInIsrael) "Shake lulav and etrog with bracha tomorrow (first day of Sukkot)." else "Shake lulav and etrog with bracha on the first and second days of Yom Tov (Diaspora); continue on Chol HaMoed per custom."}
• Festive meals in the sukkah; Ushpizin welcome guests.
• No melacha; Full Hallel and Musaf in davening.

${diasporaSecondDayNote(profile, "Sukkot")}

$opener

Havdalah when Shabbat leads into Yom Tov:
• Havdalah is recited when entering a day of lesser holiness. Shabbat is holier than Yom Tov, so when Shabbat leads into a festival, havdalah is included in that night's Kiddush — not as a full separate havdalah with spices before Kiddush.
• Order (mnemonic YaKNeHaZ per many Ashkenaz poskim): Yayin (borei pri hagafen) → Kiddush for Yom Tov → Ner (borei me'orei ha'eish — recite over the Yom Tov candles already lit on the table; do NOT pick up, move, or touch them — they are muktzeh once lit; gaze at the flames from where they stand) → Havdalah (holiday text ending bein kodesh l'kodesh, not bein kodesh l'chol) → Zeman (Shehecheyanu on the first festival night when applicable).
• Spices (besamim) are omitted for this transition.
• Before Kiddush, melacha permitted on Yom Tov but not on Shabbat: many say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh, or rely on the Vatodi'enu insert in Maariv — follow your Machzor.

$prepWhen: Yom Tov candles from a pre-existing flame; wine; festive meal ready; 48-hour candle or pilot light per your rav.

$opener

Havdalah inside Kiddush (Yaknehaz):
• When a biblical holiday begins Saturday night (including Rosh Hashana), that night's Kiddush incorporates havdalah for Shabbat. The order is: (1) wine blessing, (2) holiday Kiddush, (3) candle blessing, (4) havdalah blessing — with the Yom Tov text concluding bein kodesh l'kodesh (not bein kodesh l'chol), (5) Shehecheyanu for the festival.
• This is the YaKNeHaZ order (Yayin, Kiddush, Ner, Havdalah, Zeman) codified in Shulchan Arukh for Motzei Shabbat into Yom Tov.
• Spices are not used.
• Use the Machzor or siddur nusach for Rosh Hashana — do not rely on memory for the long havdalah text.

Before or at Maariv:
• You may say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh to begin Yom Tov-permitted activities before Kiddush, or rely on Vatodi'enu in the Amidah of Maariv — follow your community.

$prepLead:
• Have round challah, honey, apples, and symbolic foods ready for the Yom Tov meals after Shabbat (minhag).
• Confirm shofar and Musaf times for the first day(s) of Rosh Hashana after Shabbat — shofar is not blown on Shabbat itself.
• Tashlich: when the first day of Rosh Hashana is Shabbat, it is postponed to Sunday (structural rule — avoiding carrying machzorim in public without an eruv).

Candles: after Shabbat ends, light Yom Tov candles from a flame lit before Shabbat began (pre-existing flame).

$intro

$sederNights
$secondSederHachana
$omerTrigger

Set up before Yom Tov:
• Matzah — shmurah matzah for motzi/matza mitzvah (three matzot on the plate per custom)
• Maror — romaine, horseradish, or bitter herbs per your minhag
• Four cups of wine per participant (grape juice is widely used if needed — ask your rav; not the same debate as Chol HaMoed wine)
• Haggadah for each person (or shared)
• Seder plate: zeroa (shankbone), beitzah (egg), karpas, charoset, maror, chazeret
• Seder plate prep: You should ideally roast your zeroa (shankbone) on Erev Pesach day before sunset. Because the shankbone is not eaten on Seder night, roasting it after the holiday begins violates Yom Tov cooking laws. The egg (beitzah), however, is traditionally eaten during the meal, so it may legally be boiled or roasted on Yom Tov night if needed.
• Reclining (hasebha): Recline to the left when drinking the four cups and eating matzah, korech, and afikoman — do not recline while eating maror or chazeret (they symbolize slavery).
• Festive table; candles for Yom Tov

Kitchen:
• Only kosher-for-Passover food and utensils from this point
• Warm food on a blech or pre-set timer for Yom Tov meals

At the Seder: follow your Haggadah step by step — Kiddush, the order of the night, brachot, and the four cups are all laid out there. ${if (profile.isInIsrael) "One" else "Two"} Seder night(s) this Pesach.

Birkat Ha'Ilanot (בִּרְכַּת הָאִילָנוֹת — Blessing of the Trees) is recited once each Jewish year upon seeing fruit trees in bloom — thanking Hashem for the beauty and renewal of creation.

This checklist appears during the likely season for your hemisphere — a reminder to go look for blossoms, not an obligation to recite before you see them.

$whenBlock

The blessing (said once per year):
Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam shelo chiser be'olamo kelum u'vara vo beriyot tovot v'ilanot tovim lehanot bahem benei adam.
(בָּרוּךְ אַתָּה ה' אֱלֹקֵינוּ מֶלֶךְ הָעוֹלָם שֶׁלֹּא חִסֵּר בְּעוֹלָמוֹ כְּלוּם וּבָרָא בוֹ בְּרִיּוֹת טוֹבוֹת וְאִילָנוֹת טוֹבִים לֵהָנוֹת בָּהֶם בְּנֵי אָדָם)
"Blessed are You, L-rd our G-d, King of the universe, Who has withheld nothing from His world and created in it good creatures and good trees for people to enjoy."

How to fulfill it:
• Go outdoors to fruit-bearing trees — at least one tree; preferably two or more of the same or different species.
• Recite the blessing once while viewing the blossoms. Many add Tehillim 104 or other verses; follow your siddur or community custom.
• Once a year: if you said it in your local spring, you do not repeat it when traveling to another hemisphere later in the same Jewish year.
• Many communities avoid saying it on Shabbat — go during the week when you first see suitable blossoms.

What stage must the tree be in (melav'lave — מְלַבְלְבֵי)?
The rabbis require the tree to be actively flowering — not merely sprouting leaves. Shulchan Arukh O.C. 226:1 defines the Talmudic stage of melav'lave as when trees "put forth flowers" (shemotzi'in perach): you need open, visible blossoms that you can recognize as flowers. The blessing praises Hashem for creating "good trees for people to enjoy" — that response is meant to come from seeing beautiful, open blooms.

L'chatchilah (ideal): the tree has moved past the leaf-only stage and shows open flowers, but the petals have not yet fallen and fruit has not begun to form.

What does not work:
• Green leaf buds alone — invalid; do not recite the blessing on leaves without open flowers.
• After petals have dropped and fruit is forming — too late for this year's blessing.

Bedieved (after the fact): some contemporary poskim allow reciting on swollen, tightly closed flower buds that are clearly about to open when no properly blooming trees are available — ask your rav if that is your only option.

Spiritual note:
Nissan is the month of redemption; blossoming trees recall that creation itself waits to praise Hashem. Even a brief walk among flowering orchards can be a mitzvah.
important: Learn about true prophets! 🔍 The Rambam teaches: Once a prophet is established as genuine (like Moses or Isaiah), it's actually forbidden to constantly test them! Here's why: It's like repeatedly testing a proven friend - it shows a lack of trust. Instead, we should just do what they say and know it's what G-d wants!

Elevate your physical self! 🚿 Take a refreshing shower or bath with the intention of honoring G-d by serving him with a clean body. Did you know? The great sage Hillel taught that keeping our body clean is actually a mitzvah! When you care for your body with this holy intention, even something as simple as washing becomes a spiritual act! Source: (Vayikra Rabbah 34:3)

Understand true prophecy! 👥 The Rambam explains in Chapter 7: A true prophet must be wise, strong in character, and completely in control of their desires. Here's something fascinating: True prophecy only comes to someone who is in a state of joy! That's why the prophets would have musicians play before receiving prophecy. The lesson? Serving G-d with happiness opens up spiritual channels!

Protect G-d's holy Name! 📜 The Rambam teaches us in Chapter 6 that we must be extra careful with any items containing G-d's Name. Did you know? If you find a holy book or paper with G-d's Name, you should store it respectfully or bring it to a Genizah (special storage for holy items). Even if it's just one letter of G-d's Name, it must be treated with reverence. Take a moment today to properly store any holy books or papers you have!

Honor G-d's Name! 👑 The Rambam teaches us something powerful: Sanctifying G-d's Name isn't just about what you say - it's about how you live! When a Torah scholar is honest in business, kind to others, and speaks gently, people say 'Look how beautiful are the ways of Torah!' That's Kiddush Hashem! Here's your mission: Do something today that will make others think highly of G-d and His Torah. Remember: You're G-d's ambassador in this world! ✡

Strengthen your focus on G-d! 🎯 Take a moment to understand this fascinating mitzvah: The Rambam explains that even briefly entertaining thoughts about other spiritual powers besides G-d is forbidden. Here's something amazing: This isn't just about idol worship - it's about recognizing that EVERYTHING comes directly from G-d! When something good happens, don't say 'my luck' or 'my zodiac sign' - instead, remember that G-d is the only true power!

Discover G-d's unique unity! ✡ The Rambam reveals something mind-blowing in Halacha 7: G-d's oneness isn't like anything we know! A person is 'one' but has many parts (arms, legs, etc.). A group can be 'one' but made of many individuals. But G-d's oneness? Absolutely unique - no parts, no divisions, pure unity! Here's the challenge: Take a moment to contemplate this deep idea. Try to imagine a unity so complete that there's NOTHING outside of it!

Explore the mystical letter Gimmel (ג)! ✡ This amazing letter teaches us about kindness: Its shape looks like someone running - specifically, running to do good deeds! The long 'leg' of the letter reaches down, symbolizing how we should reach out to those in need. Did you know? Gimmel stands for 'Gomel' (bestowing good) - just as G-d constantly bestows goodness on us, we should bestow goodness on others! Take a moment to think: Who can you 'run' to help today? ✡

Connect to the Holy Land! 🗺️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) that G-d gave Israel to the Jewish people as an eternal inheritance. Did you know? The air of Israel is so holy that the Talmud says it makes people wise! Here's something amazing: When G-d gave us the land, He also gave us the strength to keep His commandments properly. Feeling far from Israel? Remember: Every Jew has a portion in the Holy Land - it's your spiritual home base!

Discover the power of Divine protection! 🛡️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d turned Balaam's curses into blessings. Here's something amazing: A powerful sorcerer was hired to curse the Jewish people, but G-d made him speak beautiful blessings instead! Including the famous 'How goodly are your tents, Jacob' that we say every morning! The lesson? When others try to bring you down, G-d can transform their negativity into the biggest blessings!

Explore the foundations of faith! ✡ The Rambam teaches something amazing: The very first mitzvah isn't just to believe in G-d, but to KNOW there is a G-d. How do we do this? Look at creation with wonder - from the precise orbits of planets to the complexity of a single cell! Just like you know the house you're in had a builder without seeing them, the intricate design of the universe proves there's a Creator! Take a moment to notice the incredible wisdom in G-d's creation around you.

Learn from history's mistakes! 🏜️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how even after seeing incredible miracles in the desert, our ancestors sometimes lost faith and complained. The biggest mistake? Building a golden calf just 40 days after hearing G-d's voice! Here's the powerful lesson: Even if we make big mistakes, G-d always gives us a chance to return and do better. When you're feeling down about a spiritual slip-up, remember: it's never too late to start fresh!

Do the mitzvah of experiencing both love and awe of G-d! ❤️ Watch a sunset, climb a mountain, feel the rain on your face. These wonders will blow your mind and make you realize how incredibly small and insignificant you are compared to the Creator. It's like loving a majestic lion – you're captivated by its beauty and power, but you also know you wouldn't want to mess with it. This awe-inspiring experience will fill you with love and a healthy dose of fear, reminding you of God's incredible might.

Experience the joy of freedom! 🌅 Take a moment to remember our exodus from Egypt (one of the 10 daily remembrances): We were slaves doing back-breaking labor, but G-d turned everything around with amazing miracles! Even cooler? The Egyptians who refused to pay us for our work ended up giving us their wealth when we left! This teaches us that G-d can completely transform any situation - from darkness to light, from slavery to freedom. When things look tough, remember: salvation can come in the blink of an eye!

Strengthen your trust in G-d! Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d provided manna - miraculous food that fell from heaven - every single day in the desert. Here's something amazing: Each person got exactly what they needed - no more, no less! And it tasted like whatever they wanted! The lesson? Just like G-d provided then, He provides for us now, though often in less obvious ways. When worried about livelihood, remember: G-d has infinite ways to provide for us! Don't turn to not-kosher avenues of attaining livelihood!

Relive the most epic moment in history! 🗻 Take a moment to remember when we stood at Mount Sinai (one of the 10 daily remembrances). Picture it: The entire mountain was on fire, there was thunder and lightning, and then... complete silence as we heard G-d's voice! Did you know? Our souls actually left our bodies when we heard G-d speak - it was too intense! After the first two commandments, we asked Moshe to be our intermediary. Here's something amazing: The rabbis teach that your soul was there too, and remembering this moment might very well reawaken that incredible spiritual connection!

If you have clothes or garments with wool in them, get them checked for shaatnez (a forbidden mixture of wool and linen) 🧵. If no need, learn something about shaatnez: The prohibition is linked to the story of Cain and Abel. Kayin, a farmer, brought produce (symbolized by linen) as an offering to G-d, and Hevel, a shepherd, offered animals (symbolized by wool). When G-d favored Abel's offering, it led to jealousy and conflict. Shaatnez teaches us to respect differences rather than fight them. This separation is only resolved in the Temple service, where wool and linen coexist in the priestly garments, symbolizing ultimate unity under divine purpose 👔.

Learn about the ultimate "handle with care" policy for Hashem's names! ✍️ The Torah commands "you shall not do so to Hashem your G-d," which the sages explain as a strict prohibition against erasing or destroying His holy Name. This isn't just about being careful—it’s about treating the physical representation of the Divine with the highest level of honor! 🏛️ The halacha identifies seven specific Names that are so holy they can never be erased. If a scribe accidentally writes one of these in a Torah scroll and makes a mistake, he can't just reach for an eraser; the entire section must be cut out and placed in a "Genizah" (a respectful storage for holy papers until they can be buried). 📜 This law also means you have to be mindful of where you write these Names. We avoid writing them on anything that might be thrown away or treated disrespectfully—which is exactly why you'll often see people write "G-d" with a dash or use abbreviations like 'יי' in Hebrew books. There have even been documented cases of people writing Hashem's name on blackboards in schools, and the rabbis ruled that the entire blackboard needed to be buried! 📧 Even the "holy nicknames" we use for Hashem, like "The Merciful One," must be treated with dignity, though they don't have the same strict prohibition against erasing as the core seven. 🕊️ It’s a powerful exercise in mindfulness, reminding us that even the letters on a page can carry a spark of the Infinite!
